one_line_takeaway: A reproducibility-first toolbox showing that implementation bugs, above all tokenization reachability, can swing measured jailbreak ASR by up to 28%, and packaging 12 attacks, 6 datasets, and 13 judges behind corrected, comparable APIs.
key_finding: Correctness of implementation, especially tokenization, is not a minor detail but a first-order determinant of measured robustness. Across 3000 GCG runs (750,000 prompt candidates) the improved reachability filter catches 2% more illegal sequences than existing implementations, affecting 94% of all attack runs, with up to 15% of previously-passing candidates actually unreachable in some cases. Combined tokenization corrections yield up to 28% higher ASR than baseline implementations on some models using identical weights, datasets, and attacks. The custom ragged-batch inference gives on average 2.12x better alignment with single-batch reference generations.
core_contribution: AdversariaLLM is a unified, modular Python toolbox for LLM jailbreak-robustness research, released together with a companion judging package JudgeZoo. Its distinct contribution is not a new attack or defense but corrected, comparable reference infrastructure: the authors re-implement 12 adversarial attack algorithms (spanning discrete, continuous, and hybrid input spaces, single- and multi-turn), integrate 6 datasets (harmfulness and over-refusal), and consolidate 13 evaluation judges in JudgeZoo behind a unified API. The central technical claim is that subtle implementation bugs in existing toolboxes materially distort reported results, and that fixing them (tokenization filtering, chat templates, batched generation) changes measured robustness substantially. The four modules (datasets, models, attacks, evaluation) communicate through standardized APIs and are designed for extensibility. Note: the safety benchmarks discussed in Section 2 (HarmBench, JailbreakBench, StrongREJECT, XSTest, OR-Bench, CleverHans, RobustBench, garak, PandaGuard) are cited prior work the toolbox wraps or compares against, not contributions of this paper.
threat_model: The paper is a research/evaluation framework operating within the LLM jailbreak threat model: adversarial inputs (adversarial suffixes, multi-turn escalation, embedding-space perturbations, high-volume sampling) that attempt to make safety-aligned LLMs produce harmful content, plus the converse over-refusal failure on benign prompts. The toolbox itself is white-box and currently focused on open-weight models loaded via Hugging Face; the authors note black-box/API attacks and ad-hoc defenses (e.g., SmoothLLM) are not yet supported. A recurring implicit adversary in the paper is the hosted-model API surface: token-space attacks may find token sequences that are unreachable from any text input once messages are flattened and tokenized, so a faithful threat model must verify reachability of the full tokenized conversation.
limitations: Stated by the authors: (1) attacks may still produce slightly varying results across different hardware even with fixed seeds, due to dynamic chat templates and non-deterministic batched behavior / numerical differences; (2) because many included attacks are white-box, the toolbox is currently limited to open-weight models, with black-box/API attack support planned but not yet implemented; (3) it assumes defenses are built into the model weights and does not yet support ad-hoc/inference-time defenses such as SmoothLLM. Reviewer-observed: the headline empirical validation (28% ASR gain, per-step ASR curves) is demonstrated mainly on GCG against Llama-2-7B-Instruct, so generality of the ASR-gain magnitude across the full 12-attack/6-dataset matrix is asserted more than exhaustively shown; the generation-correctness factor is model-dependent (one of six models, Phi-3-mini, actually regressed to 0.84x).

datasets_used: HarmBench Behaviors (520 harmful prompts, 6 risk categories; non-copyright subset used for GCG ASR experiments)
StrongREJECT small (60 prompts) and full (313 prompts)
XSTest (250 curated safe/benign prompts for over-refusal)
OR-Bench-Hard-1k (1,000 auto-generated benign prompts likely to trigger false refusals)
A harmful/harmless prompt dataset interface for computing refusal directions
quantitative_results: Up to 28% higher ASR than baseline implementations from correctness (tokenization) fixes alone, on some models with identical weights/datasets/attacks
Tokenization filter validated over 3000 GCG runs / 750,000 prompt candidates; catches 2% more illegal sequences, affecting 94% of attack runs, up to 15% more in some cases

Batched generation alignment (Table 5): average 2.12x more tokens matching the greedy unbatched baseline; per-model factors 1.78 (Ministral-8B), 244.33 (zephyr-7b-r2d2), 0.84 (Phi-3-mini, a regression), 1.27 (Qwen2-7B), 2.24 (Llama-2-7b-chat), 7.05 (Llama-3.1-8B)
Llama-3.1-8B matches the reference 256-token generation 71% of the time vs 19% for the baseline model.generate
Coverage counts: 12 attack algorithms, 6 datasets, 13 judges (JudgeZoo)
baselines_compared: nanoGCG / GCG reference implementation (tokenization filter and ASR comparison)
HarmBench framework (feature comparison Table 1; tokenization baseline)
Hugging Face default model.generate (ragged-batch generation correctness, Table 5)
